english: well again); sróg-gi (srog gi) ká-ba (ka ba) n. of a vein; sróg-gi (srog gi) snyíṅ-po (snying po) Mil.? Comp. *sróg-skyób* (*srog skyob*) W. deliverer, redeemer, saviour. - sróg-k'uṅ (srog khung) Mil. the deep cut or stab, by which Tibetan butchers kill animals (Huc I, 443), sróg-k'uṅ (srog khung) byéd-pa (byed pa) to stab in this manner. - sróg-čán (srog can), srog-ldán (srog ldan) having life, living, alive. - srog-čágs (srog chags) animated being, mí-la (mi la) sógs-pai (sogs pai) srog-čágs (srog chags) t'ams-čád (thams cad) all men and other living beings Dzl., srog-t'ág (srog thag) = dpyaṅ-t'ág (dpyang thag). - srog-bdág (srog bdag) čén-po (chen po) = pe-dkár (pe dkar) Glr. - srog-méd (srog med) lifeless, inanimate. - srog-rtsá (srog rtsa) 'root of life, vein of life', aorta S.g., chiefly used rhetor. and fig. - srog-lén (srog len) deadly, fatal Lt. - srog-śíṅ (srog shing) axle, axle-tree; mčod-rtén-gyi (mchod rten gyi) srog-śíṅ (srog shing) the pole in a Chodten; fig. prop, séms-kyi (sems kyi) srog-śíṅ (srog shing) Mil.

pf. bsraṅs (bsrangs), fut. bsraṅ (bsrang), imp. sroṅ(s) (srong(s)), bsraṅ (bsrang), W. *sráṅ-če* (*srang ce*), to make straight, to straighten, yón-po (yon po) what is awry, crooked Lex., gzer (gzer) sróṅ-ba (srong ba) to beat out nails; to equal Sch.; sku dráṅ-por (sku drang por) bsráṅs-te (bsrangs te) (he sat) straight and erect, cf. also sraṅ (srang) and bsráṅ-po (bsrang po). - W.: *sráṅ-te (*srang te) ča (cha) dúg-ga (dug ga) 'i-ru ('i ru) dad* (dad*) will he pass straight through or does he stay here?
Srongtsangampo, n. of the most famous king of Tibet, a contemporary of Mohammed; he introduced the Tibetan letters, and was the chief promoter of Buddhism and its literature.

dusk of the evening, twilight, *środ rub* (*shrod rub*) W. the dusk of evening draws near, it is getting dusky, srod (srod) daṅ (dang) t'o-raṅs (tho rangs) in the evening and morning Lt.; srod byíṅ (srod bying) soṅ (song) night has set in; srod yol (srod yol) soṅ (song) id., viz. the time about 11 o'clock at night C.; srod-la (srod la) in the dusk of evening Mil. - srod-k'or-p'ag (srod khor phag) Cs.(?) the k'yim (khyim) of evening-twilight, v. nyín-žag (nyin zhag). - srod-loṅ (srod long) dayblind, nyctalops, seeing better in a mild than in a bright light.

usage, custom, common use, habitual practice, habit, der (der) yi-gei (yi gei) srol (srol) méd-pas (med pas) as the art of writing is not yet in use there Glr.; sṅár-srol (sngar srol) bzáṅ-po-la (bzang po la) dgoṅs (dgongs) śig (shig) keep in mind the good old customs Glr.; srol (srol) čágs-pa (chags pa), sról-du (srol du) gyúr-ba (gyur ba) Cs., sról-du (srol du) _tsúd-pa ('tsud pa) to grow into a habit, to become the custom (of a person, a country); srol (srol) _dzúgs-pa ('dzugs pa) to introduce a practice Glr.; srol (srol) gtód-pa (gtod pa) Lex. prob. the same; bka-sról (bka' srol) = srol (srol), but at the same time expressive of reverence for the originator of the custom Zam.; p'yag-sról (phyag srol) Mil. is said to be a respectful expression for lag-lén-gyi (lag len gyi) srol (srol) (?); legs-pai (legs pai) dpe-srol (dpe srol) btsug-ste (btsug ste) Glr. having introduced good customs for imitation; t'ob-sról (thob srol) claim, title, right, founded on old custom.
1. v. sro-ba (sro ba). - 2. Cs. sbst. = srod (srod) twilight, dusk of evening, mún-sros-pa (mun sros pa) dusky, dark; Glr.: sa srós-nas (sa sros nas) when it grew dark.
a large iron pan for parching grain, slaṅ-dregs (slang dregs) soot adhering to a pan.

adj., also slá-mo (sla mo) 1. thin, of fluids (opp. to ská-ba (ska ba), túg-po (tug po), réṅs-pa (rengs pa)) W. *lan-te*. - 2. easy, opp. to dká-bo (dka' bo) difficult, śés-pa slá-ba ma yin (shes pa sla ba ma yin) knowledge is not easily obtained Dzl.; usually with the supine: rig-par slao (rig par slao) it may easily be found out Dzl., or with the root of the verb: go-slá (go sla) easy to be comprehended. II. vb., v. slé-ba (sle ba).

fur-coat, sgo-slóg (sgo slog) Mil., more corr. dgo-slóg (dgo slog), hunting-coat, made of the skin of an antelope; spyaṅ-slág (spyang slag), W. *śaṅ-lag* (*shang lag*) fur-coat of a wolf's skin; tsar-slág (tsar slag) coat of lamb's skins; ras-slág (ras slag) prob.: a fur-coat covered with calico Glr.
1. v. sub sla-ṅa (sla nga). - 2. v. slóṅ-ba (slong ba).

eleg. = p'yí (phyi) I, II, IV, 1. slad-ról (slad rol) hind part, back part Lex., slád-bźin-du (slad bzhin du) or slád-bźin-par (slad bzhin par) behind, e.g. _bráṅ-ba ('brang ba) to walk behind one, slád-sa (slad sa) = p'yí-sa (phyi sa) dung Bhar. - 2. after, slád-na (slad na) c. genit. = _óg-tu ('og tu) Dzl.; slád-nas (slad nas) adv. afterwards, hereafter, subsequently Lex. and C.; slád-kyi (slad kyi) subsequent, later, posterior; slád-ma (slad ma) Cs.:
